title: DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY 114,529-5% (1986) BRIEF NOTES The Specificity of the CAMP Receptor Mediating Activation of Adenylate Cyclase in Dictyostekm discoideum ANNETHEIBERT,MARIAPALMISANO,BERNDJASTORFF,*ANDPETERDEVREOTES

DEVELOPMENTAL 
BIOLOGY 
114,529-5% 
(1986) 
BRIEF NOTES 
The Specificity of the CAMP Receptor Mediating Activation 
of Adenylate Cyclase in Dictyostekm 
discoideum 
ANNETHEIBERT,MARIAPALMISANO,BERNDJASTORFF,*ANDPETERDEVREOTES 
Department 
of Biological 
Chemistly/, 
The Johns Hopkins 
University 
School of Medicine, 
Baltimcrre, 
Mar&nd

of

of Bremen, D-ZXW-Bremen-33, 
Federal 
Republic of Germany 
Received July 31, 1985; accepted in revised form November 
4, 1985 
In fiotyostelium 
discoideum amoebae, binding of cyclic AMP (CAMP) to surface receptors 
elicits numerous responses 
including 
chemotaxis, 
cyclic GMP (cGMP) 
accumulation, 
and activation 
of adenylate 
cyclase. The specificity 
of the 
surface CAMP receptor 
which mediates activation 
of adenylate 
cyclase and CAMP secretion 
was determined 
by testing 
the relative 
effectiveness 
of a series of 10 CAMP analogs. Each of the 10 analogs elicited CAMP secretion, 
chemotaxis, 
and cGMP accumulation 
in the same dose range. The order of potency for eliciting 
these responses (CAMP > 2’-H-CAMP 
> Ni-O-CAMP 
> cAMPS(Sp) 
> 6-Cl-CAMP 
> cAMPN(CH&(Sp) 
> 3’-NH-CAMP 
> 8-Br-CAMP 
> cAMPS(Rp) 
> cAMPN(CH&(Rp)) 
matches that for binding 
to the major cell surface CAMP binding 
sites and differs 
from that of 
the cell surface phosphodiesterase 
and the major intracellular 
CAMP binding 
protein. 
0 Is86 Academic 
Press, 1~. 
INTRODUCTION 
In Dictyostelium 
starvation 
initiates 
development, 
whereby lo6 amoebae aggregate into a multicellular 
structure which differentiates 
into stalks and spores 
(Devreotes, 1982; Gerisch, 1982; Van Haastert and Kon- 
ijn, 1982). Spontaneously synthesized by cells in aggre- 
gation centers, cyclic AMP (CAMP) diffuses to adjacent 
cells, binds to surface receptors, and activates adenylate 
cyclase, sustaining the signal relay. The resulting waves 
provide gradients for chemotaxis. Evidence indicates 
that CAMP regulates gene expression (Darmon et al., 
1977; Sampson et al, 1978; Chisholm et al, 1984), mor- 
phogenesis (Sussman, 1982) and differentiation 
(Gross 
et al, 1981). 
Other responses concurrent with signaling and che- 
motaxis include a rise in intracellular 
cyclic GMP 
(cGMP) (Mato et al., 19’77a,b), a decrease in extracellular 
pH (Malchow et al, 1978), an increase in cellular Ca2+ 
and decrease in cellular K+ (Wick et al., 1978; Bumann 
et al, 1984), phosphorylation 
of myosin and the major 
surface CAMP receptor (Berlot et aZ., 1985, Lubs-Hauke- 
ness and Klein, 1985), and an increase in vesicles near 
the cell surface (Maeda and Gerisch, 1977). These re- 
ceptors (Mato et ah, 1977a; Mato and Konijn, 1977; Van 
Haastert et aZ., 1982; Van Haastert, 1983; Van Haastert 
and Kien, 1983), phosphodiesterase (Van Haastert et ah, 
1983), and binding to the major cytoplasmic binding 
protein (De Wit et ah, 1982). 
MATERIALS 
AND 
METHODS 
Measurement of CAMP signaling responses. Dictyoste- 
Zium discoideum strain NC-4 cells were grown and 
[3H]cAMP secretion monitored as previously described 
(Devreotes et al, 1979; Devreotes and Steck, 1980). Cells 
were developed at 7°C for 16 hr or 22°C for 3-5 hr in 
the dark. 
Chemicals. Cyclic AMP and analogs c,l f, and i were 
purchased from Sigma Company (St. Louis, MO.). The 
synthesis of d, h, and b has been previously described 
(Van Haastert, 1983; Van Haastert and Kien, 1983; Jas- 
torff and Friest, 1974). Analogs e, g, j, k were synthesized 
in Dr. JastorfI’s laboratory. rH]adenosine was from ICN 
(Irvine, Calif.). 
Data analysis. Total [3H]cAMP secreted determined 
at each concentration was plotted versus log [stimulus]. 
sponses are all initiated by binding of CAMP to the cell 
surface, yet it is not known whether one class of CAMP

Responses were normalized to the saturated response 
except when saturation was not achieved when responses 
were normalized to that of a 100 nMcAMP control stim- 
ulus. The I& is the concentration of CAMP or analog 
that elicits 50% of the maximum response. To facilitate 
the comparison of signaling data with that reported for 
other responses, data was converted to R. R = log (con- 
centration of analog eliciting half-maximal 
response/ 
concentration of CAMP eliciting half-maximal response). 
RESULTS 
Cells labeled with [3H]adenosine and developed to ag- 
gregation competence were placed on filters, and stim- 
ulated with CAMP or a CAMP analog. The cells respond 
by activating adenylate cyclase, the newly synthesized 
CAMP is rapidly secreted, then the secretion rate de- 
clines to prestimulus levels (Devreotes et aZ., 1979) as 
the cells adapt to the stimulus. The total amount of 
[3H]cAMP secreted (the CAMP signaling response) de- 
pends on the CAMP stimulus concentration, saturating 
at 100 nMcAMP (>lO &lfdecreases the maximum). The 
effectiveness of CAMP and 10 analogs (listed in Table 1) 
in eliciting CAMP secretion was determined. Dose-re- 
sponse data for these analogs are shown in Fig. 1. Mod- 
ification of the CAMP molecule at different positions in- 
creased the dose required to elicit a half maximal re- 
sponse by varying degrees. For example, the 2’-deoxy 
CAMP analog (c) was 20-fold less effective than CAMP. 
Substitution 
of Cl for H at the 6 position (f) caused a 
500-fold reduction in effectiveness. The concentrations 
required to produce 50% of the maximal response (I&) 
for the analogs are listed in Table 1. 
Modifications change the effectiveness in eliciting a 
signaling response, but the shape of the dose-response 
curve is similar for each analog and all responses appear 
to saturate (where sufficiently high concentrations of 
analog could be tested). The maximal response achieved 
was similar to that produced by a saturating 
dose of 
CAMP. Furthermore, at appropriate doses, responses of 
equivalent magnitude had identical kinetics, regardless 
of the analog used to stimulate. 
R values (R = log of the concentration of analog elic- 
iting half-maximal 
response divided by the concentra- 
tion of CAMP which elicited a half-maximal 
response) 
calculated for signaling are presented in Table 1 together 
with the R values calculated from previously reported 
data for the rise in intracellular 
cGMP, chemotaxis, 
surface receptor binding antagonism (summarized in 
Van Haastert, 1983; Van Haastert and Kien, 1983), sub- 
strate specificity of the CAMP phosphodiesterase (Van 
Haastert et al., 1983), and cytoplasmic CAMP binding 
activity (De Wit et al., 1982). A graphical comparison of 
the signaling R to the other R values is shown in Fig, 2. 
Since cAMPS(Rp) and cAMPN(CH,)z(Rp) elicited only 
small signaling responses but not the chemotactic or 
cGMP response, the data for these two analogs were 
omitted from this graphical comparison. From Fig. 2, it 
is evident that there is a high degree of correlation be- 
tween the potency of an analog in eliciting a CAMP sig- 
naling response and each of the other three responses. 
Linear regression analysis yields lines with slopes and 
intercepts of 0.80 and 0.060 for signaling compared with 
binding, 0.90 and -0.026 compared with chemotaxis, and 
0.73 and 0.182 compared with cGMP accumulation. The 
correlation coefficients for these lines are r = 0.97, r 
= 0.86, and r = 0.99, respectively. This data shows that 
with the exception of the two Rp analogs, the analogs 
elicit the various responses with approximately the same 
order of effectiveness and at the same concentrations. 
There is little correlation between the R values for 
signaling compared to surface phosphodiesterase sub- 
strate activity 
or binding to the intracellular

binding protein shown in Fig. 2b. The slopes, intercepts, 
and correlation coefficients are 0.48, -0.16 and 0.52 for 
the comparison of signaling versus phosphodiesterase, 
and -0.20,2.0, and -0.14 for signaling versus the intra- 
cellular binding protein. Chemotaxis, cGMP accumula- 
tion and the signaling response are mediated by a similar 
receptor which has very different specificity than either 
the CAMP phosphodiesterase or the intracellular 
CAMP 
binding protein. 
DISCUSSION 
Previous reports indicate that cGMP accumulation 
and chemotaxis are mediated by the same receptor (Mato 
et al., 1977a,b; Van Haastert, 1983; Van Haastert and 
Kien, 1983). We have determined the specificity of the 
receptor that mediates the CAMP signaling response. 
The comparison of this data with that determined by 
other groups for binding antagonism, chemotaxis, and 
cGMP accumulation shows that the specificity of the 
receptor mediating each process is nearly identical. A 
minor discrepancy was found with the Rp analogs, 
cAMPS(Rp) and cAMPN(CH&(Rp) 
which reportedly 
elicit no chemotactic or cGMP response (Van Haastert, 
1983; Van Haastert and Kien, 1983). These Rp analogs 
elicited small signaling responses (Fig. lj, k) at high 
concentrations. Contamination 
of the Rp isomer with 
the Sp isomer or CAMP could account for this discrep- 
ancy. This seems unlikely since we used the same initial 
preparations as used by Van Haastert (1983), however, 
we cannot rule out that in subsequent handling our 
preparations were slightly 
degraded to CAMP. Alter- 
natively, detection of very weak chemotactic and cGMP 
responses may be difficult. 
The dose-response curves show that signaling re- 
sponses saturate at high concentrations and the mag- 
nitude of the maximal response is the same as that ob- 
tained with CAMP. The signaling response, at least, ap- 
pears to depend on the fractional 
receptor occupancy 
rather than the rate of receptor association since the 
magnitude of the maximal response to the lower affinity 
analogs was the same as that obtained with CAMP. 
It has been proposed that there are several subclasses 
of surface receptors, a minor set is linked to cGMP ac- 
cumulation and a major set to activation of adenylate 
cyclase (Van Haastert and De Wit, 1984). If this is so, 
our study indicates that the two classes have identical 
specificity. Furthermore, photoaffinity labeling of intact 
cells reveals only a single major CAMP binding species, 
which migrates as a doublet between 40 and 43,000 Da 
(Theibert et aZ., 1984) is present on intact cells. A minor 
amount ~5% of the [32P]8-N, CAMP is incorporated into 
a few other bands. Until the specificity of these is de- 
termined, we cannot rule out that they could mediate 
other responses. 
Exogenous CAMP profoundly influences the develop- 
mental regulation of a host of gene products. The in- 
duction of the CAMP receptor itself, adenylate cyclase, 
and the membrane phosphodiesterase require pulses of 
low concentrations of CAMP every 6 min from the onset 
of development (Darmon et al, 1977). Constant low levels 
of CAMP suppress the expression of these gene products 
(Klein and Darmon, 1977) and enhance the induction of 
soluble phosphodiesterase and suppression of its inhib- 
itor (Tsang and Coukell, 1977). Later in development, 
high concentrations of CAMP induce expression of pres- 
talk specific mRNAs and prevent the rapid degradation 
of these RNAs when cells are disaggregated (Landfear 
and Lodish, 1980). The specificity data presented here 
provide information 
necessary for understanding 
the 
adenylate cyclase and phosphodiesterase inhibitor of Dictyostelium
